note: The Peak Tram and the Sky Terrace have lift access and the terminal is accessible, but the surrounding Peak trails and some viewpoints involve slopes and steps; the main terrace view is manageable. The tram itself is steep.
Historic harbour ferries with ramped/level boarding at the piers and accessible deck space; a cheap, scenic, accessible harbour crossing. Confirm the pier ramp.

place: Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade / Avenue of Stars
note: A flat, paved, fully step-free harbourfront walk with the best skyline views and the Symphony of Lights vantage; easy and pleasant for everyone.
The Ngong Ping cable car is accessible and the village is flat, but the Big Buddha itself sits atop 268 steps with no lift; you can view it from the base. Cable car and village yes, statue climb no.
Modern museum with full lift access, step-free galleries and accessible toilets; easy throughout.
A lively street market that is flat but extremely crowded and tight between stalls, with uneven road surface; navigable slowly but a squeeze for wheels and strollers.
An atmospheric historic temple with a step at the entrance and incense-dense interior on Hollywood Road's slope; partly accessible with a small threshold. The surrounding streets are steep.
A large temple complex with ramped accessible routes to the main altar areas, though some terraces have steps; broadly more accessible than the older temples.
A landscaped park in Central with lifts, ramps and mostly flat paved paths (some terraced sections); an accessible green break amid the towers.
The world's longest outdoor escalator system climbs the hillside; escalators aren't wheelchair-usable and the district is steep, though it's a famous stroll for the able-bodied. Not accessible.
A crowded, flat street market with tightly packed stalls and uneven road; hard to navigate with wheels or a stroller at busy times.
